worth_it_explanation: The attribution model on a conversion action decides how credit gets split across the campaigns a member saw before converting, and on LinkedIn that path usually runs across several touches and several weeks. The check is quick. Each conversion action's setting is visible in Campaign Manager, so the auditor can read the model in a few clicks and compare it against how the account actually wants credit assigned. When one conversion action uses last touch each campaign and another uses last touch last campaign, the reported numbers stop lining up, and a model chosen by default rather than on purpose can understate the campaigns that opened the relationship. Fixing it is a setting change on the conversion action, not a rebuild.
intro: On LinkedIn, each conversion action carries an attribution model that determines how credit is assigned across the campaigns a member interacted with inside the conversion window. The two choices are last touch each campaign, which credits every campaign that served an impression to the converting member, and last touch last campaign, which credits only the final campaign. These checks confirm that the model on each conversion action was chosen deliberately against a multi-touch B2B buying cycle and that related conversion actions use the model consistently.

audit_checks.title: The attribution model was never reviewed against the buying cycle
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A model left at its default can credit only the last campaign a member touched, which understates the campaigns that started the relationship weeks earlier. Optimization decisions and budget shifts then run from a credit split that does not reflect how the buying cycle actually unfolded.

educate.base:
LinkedIn lets each conversion action set its own attribution model, which controls how credit for a conversion is shared across the campaigns a member interacted with inside the conversion window. The two options are last touch each campaign, which gives credit to every campaign that served the converting member an impression in the window, and last touch last campaign, which gives all the credit to the final campaign. The model is chosen when the conversion action is created and is rarely revisited afterward.

A B2B purchase on LinkedIn usually involves several touches across weeks, often spanning awareness, consideration, and a final lower-funnel campaign. If the model credits only the last campaign, the campaigns that opened and warmed the relationship show fewer conversions than they contributed, and budget can drift toward the closing campaign while the campaigns feeding it look weaker than they are.

You may want to confirm that the model on each conversion action was a deliberate choice rather than a default left in place. The right model depends on how the account wants to assign credit. Last touch each campaign suits a conversion action tied to multiple campaigns across the funnel, while last touch last campaign suits a conversion action tied to a single campaign.

A few signs point to a model that was never reviewed:
  • The conversion action is associated with several campaigns yet credits only the last one
  • No one on the account can say why the current model was selected
  • The conversion window is short relative to a sales cycle that runs for weeks, so multi-touch credit rarely has a chance to apply
You may want to read the model alongside the conversion window since a model that shares credit across campaigns only helps when the window is wide enough to capture the earlier touches.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a considered B2B store, where a purchase follows research and several visits rather than a single session, last touch each campaign keeps the upper-funnel campaigns visible in the conversion count. You may want to confirm the model matches the length of the consideration path rather than crediting only the final click.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For the lead-capture case that dominates LinkedIn advertising, a form fill often follows several content and consideration touches. You may want to confirm the model on the lead conversion action reflects how credit should be shared across those touches, not just the campaign that delivered the final impression.

learn_more.source_title: Set up Conversion Tracking for Insight Tag conversions in Campaign Manager

investigate.intro: Confirm the attribution model on each conversion action in Campaign Manager and check it against how the account wants credit assigned across the buying cycle.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: In Campaign Manager, open the 'Analyze' menu and select 'Conversion tracking' to list the account's conversion actions.
Open a conversion action and note its attribution model, either last touch each campaign or last touch last campaign, along with its conversion window.
Review which campaigns the conversion action is associated with. If it spans several campaigns across the funnel yet credits only the last one, note the mismatch.
Ask the account owner why the current model was chosen. A model no one can account for is a sign it was left at the default rather than selected for the buying cycle.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per conversion action, the attribution model, the conversion window, the campaigns associated with it, and whether the model was a deliberate choice. A conversion action whose model fits how the account wants credit assigned across its campaigns passes. A conversion action that spans several campaigns yet credits only the last one, or whose model no one can account for, is a fail recorded with the conversion action name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the attribution model, conversion window, and associated campaigns for each conversion action above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the attribution model on {{site_name}}'s conversion actions was set at creation and never reviewed against the buying cycle. Several conversion actions span campaigns across the funnel yet credit only the final campaign, so the awareness and consideration campaigns that open the relationship show fewer conversions than they contribute, and budget can drift toward the closing campaign. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the model on each conversion action against how credit should be shared across the path, selecting last touch each campaign where a conversion action is tied to multiple campaigns, and confirming the conversion window is wide enough for the earlier touches to count. After the change, recheck the conversion count by campaign to confirm the upper-funnel campaigns are credited.

audit_checks.title: The attribution model is inconsistent across conversion actions
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When related conversion actions use different attribution models, their conversion counts are built on different rules and cannot be compared on the same footing. Blended reporting across them is misleading, and decisions made from that blend lean on numbers that do not share a definition.

educate.base:
Because LinkedIn sets the attribution model per conversion action, an account can end up with a mix, where one conversion action uses last touch each campaign and another uses last touch last campaign. Each model is valid on its own, but the two count credit differently. Last touch each campaign can credit several campaigns for one conversion, while last touch last campaign credits exactly one.

When two conversion actions that feed the same report use different models, their numbers rest on different rules. Summing them, or comparing one against the other, treats counts that were never measured the same way as if they were. A campaign that looks efficient on one conversion action and inefficient on another may differ only because the two actions split credit differently, not because performance differs.

You may want to confirm that conversion actions intended to be read together share a deliberate, consistent model. Differences are fine when they are intentional and the reason is documented, for example a single-campaign action that genuinely warrants last touch last campaign. The problem is an undocumented mix that surfaces only when the numbers fail to reconcile.

A few signs point to an inconsistent setup:
  • Conversion actions that roll up into the same report use different attribution models
  • Two conversion actions with similar purposes disagree on their model with no recorded reason
  • Blended conversion totals do not reconcile against the sum of the individual actions
You may want to settle on one model per group of related conversion actions, or document the exception where a difference is intended.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a considered B2B store, purchase and add-to-cart style conversion actions are often read together, so a model that differs between them makes the funnel report hard to trust. You may want to confirm the conversion actions in one funnel report share a model.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For the lead-capture case that dominates LinkedIn advertising, a form-fill action and a downstream qualified-lead action are frequently compared, and a model that differs between them distorts the comparison. You may want to confirm the lead conversion actions in one report use the same model, or document why one differs.

investigate.intro: Compare the attribution model across the conversion actions that are read or reported together and flag any undocumented mix.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: In Campaign Manager, open the 'Analyze' menu and select 'Conversion tracking' to list every conversion action.
For each conversion action, record its attribution model and note which report or rollup it feeds.
Group the conversion actions that are read together and compare their models. Flag any group where the models differ.
For each difference, ask whether it was intentional and whether the reason is documented. An undocumented mix within one report is the finding to record.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per group of conversion actions that are read together, the attribution model on each and whether any difference is intentional and documented. A group whose conversion actions share a consistent model, or whose differences are deliberate and recorded, passes. A group with an undocumented mix of models is a fail recorded with the conversion action names and the models in conflict.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the attribution model for each conversion action above, grouped by the report it feeds, to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s conversion actions use a mix of attribution models without a documented reason. Some actions credit every campaign that touched the member while others credit only the final campaign, so their conversion counts rest on different rules and cannot be compared or summed on the same footing, which makes the blended reporting misleading. {{voice_recommend}} settling on one attribution model for each group of conversion actions that are read together, or documenting the exception where a difference is intended, so the numbers in a single report share one definition. After the change, confirm the blended totals reconcile against the individual conversion actions.
